flashcards.back: A resizable array that automatically grows/shrinks during runtime. Examples: C++ vector, Java ArrayList, Python list.

O(1) amortized

Occasionally O(n) during resize, but spread across all operations, average is constant.

1. Total copies < n for n inserts
2. At most 50% wasted space
3. Gives O(1) amortized append
4. Simple to implement

Size: Number of elements currently stored
Capacity: Total allocated space

Always: size ≤ capacity

When size < capacity/4

Not capacity/2 to avoid thrashing (repeated grow/shrink at boundary).

flashcards.front: Time Complexity: Insert at arbitrary position
flashcards.back:
O(n)

Must shift elements to make space, regardless of resize.

Dynamic Array: O(1) access, O(n) insert/delete
Linked List: O(n) access, O(1) insert/delete (with pointer)

Dynamic Array is cache-friendly, Linked List has no resize overhead.

Factor 2×: 50% max waste, fewer resizes
Factor 1.5×: 33% max waste, more resizes
Factor 1.25×: 20% max waste, even more resizes

Most use 1.5-2×

1. O(1) random access
2. O(1) amortized append
3. Cache-friendly (contiguous)
4. No pointer overhead per element

1. O(n) insert/delete at arbitrary positions
2. Memory overhead (capacity > size)
3. Occasional O(n) resize (bad for real-time)
